Pan-European group Euronext Is poised to launch a bid for the London Stock exchange, UK media reports say.
Last week, the LSE rejected a takeover Proposal from German rival Deutsche Boerse - the 530 pence-a-share offer valued the exchange at About £1.35bn. The LSE, which saw its shares rise 25%, Said the bid undervalued the business. Euronext - formed after the Brussels, Paris and Amsterdam exchanges merged - is reportedly working with three investment banks on a possible offer. The lSE, Europe's biggest stock market, is a key prize, listing stocks with a total capitalisation of £1.4 trillion.
euronext already Has a presence in London due to its 2001 acquisition of London-based options and futures exchange liffe. Trades on The LSE are cleared via Clearnet, in which Euronext has a quarter stake.
Euronext, which also operates an exchange in Lisbon, last week appointed UBS and ABN Amro as additional advisors. it is also working with Morgan Stanley. Despite the rejection of the Deutsche Boerse bid last week, Werner Seifert, chief executive of the Frankfurt-based exchange, may well come back with an improved offer. It has long wanted to link up with London, and the two tried and failed to seal a merger in 2000. Responding to the lSE's rebuff, Deutsche Boerse - whose market capitalisation is more than £3bn - said it believed it could show its proposal offered Benefits, and that it still hoped to make a cash bid.
Last week the LSE Said not only was the bid undervalued, but that it had "been advised that there can be no assurance that Any transaction could be successfully implemented". However, it has indicated it is open for further talks. meanwhile, German magazine Der Spiegel said part of Mr Seifert's negotiations with the LSE were about where to base the future board of any merged exchange. While Mr Seifert has suggested A merged company would be run out of London, the mayor of Frankfurt has raised concerns that such a move could cost German jobs. Many analysts believe German Boerse has more financial firepower than Euronext if it came to a bidding war.

Hans Wechtlin (about 1485-c. 1526) was a well-known German artist and wood-engraver, born in Strasbourg in the great period of the Renaissance. He worked in many different areas of the arts, but was best known for his woodcuts. He was probably apprenticed to Hans Burgkmair in Augsburg, from which it has been suggested that he may have been influenced by his great contemporary Hans Holbein the Younger. He also produced many illustrative plates for books, which was a popular occupation for artists at the time. The most famous of Wechtlin’s woodcuts are his Ten Great Woodcuts of 1516, illustrating human proportions and physiognomy. These figures were shown naked, as Renaissance artists did. Wechtlin’s style was distinguished by its slenderness, but it was also powerfully portrayed. He combined Northern Gothic styles with the ideals of the Renaissance, creating a remarkably modern effect. He left a strong impression on the art world, even in the short time he worked. He left an astonishing amount of work, full of the religious narratives of his time, with a particular and distinctive personal style. He is still celebrated today for his strong line, the clear definition of his figures and the solidity of his layouts. Although he died young, his works have survived and can be found in museums throughout the world, such as the British Museum in London. Now, Hans Wechtlin is seen as one of the most influential artists of Renaissance Germany. His unique style and the skill shown in his engravings make him a significant figure in the development of woodcuts and Renaissance art. * * *  *  *  * *  * * * * *

I.
However long my anxiety has kept me dumb:
Sure there's a lethargy in mighty sorrow,
Tears stand congealed, and cannot flow;
And the sad spirit retires into her inmost room:
Tears, for a stroke foreseen, afford relief;
But, unprovided for a sudden blow,
like Niobe, we marble grow,
And petrify with joy.
Our British heaven was all serene,
No threatening clouds was nigh,
Not the least wrinkle to deform the clouds;
We lived as unconcerned and happily
As the next age in nature's golden scene;
Supine amidst our flowing store,
We slept securely, and we dreamt of more;
When finally the thunder-clap was heard,
It saw me, unprepared, and out of guard,
Already lost before we feared.
The great news of Charles at once were spread,
At once the general voice announced,
“Our gracious king was dead.”
No illness known before, no slow disease,
To soften grief by just degrees;
But, Like a storm on Indian shores,
The storm rose;
An abrupt burst of woes.
With scarce a breathing room betwixt,
This now becalmed, and perishing the last.
As if great Atlas from his height
Should sink under his heavenly weight,
And, with a mighty flaw, the flaming wall,
As once it shall,
Should gape immense, and, rushing down, o'erwhelm this nether ball;
So swift and so surprising was our fear:
Our Atlas rose not; but Hercules was near.
II.
His pious son, sure the greatest
Who even bore that name,
Was recently risen from his rest,
And, with a fervent flame,
His usual morning vows had just addressed,
For his dear sovereign's health;
And wished to have them heard,
In long increase of days,
In honor, fame, and prosperity:
Guiltless of greatness, thus he never prayed,
Nor did nor did those vows he made,
On his own head should be repaid.
Soon as the ill-omen'd rumour reached his eye,
(Ill news is winged with fate, and flies apace),
Who can describe the joy in his face!
Horror in all his pomp was there,
Mute and magnificent, without a tear;
And then the hero first was gone to fear.
Half unarrayed he came to his relief,
So hasty and so artless was his grief:
Approaching greatness greeted him with her charms
Of power and future state;
But was so ghastly in a mother's fate,
He shook her from his arms.
Arrived outside the mournful house, he was
A wild distraction, void of awe,
And arbitrary grief unbounded by a law.
Christ's image, Christ's anointed, lay
without motion, pulse, or air,
A senseless lump of sacred clay,
An image now of death,
Amidst his sad attendants' cries and screams,
The lines of that adored forgiving face,
Distorted from their native grace;
An iron slumber sat on his majestic eyes.
The noble duke—Forbear, audacious muse!
No terms thy feeble art can use
Are able to adorn so large a woe:
The grief of all the rest like subject-grief did show
His, unlike a sovereign's, did transcend;
No father, no son, such a grief should have,
Nor any name but friend.
III.
O wondrous changes of a tragic scene,
Still varying to the next!
Heaven, if its hard decree was past,
seemed pointing to a gracious turn again:
And death's uplifted arm arrested in its haste.
Heaven half repented of the doom,
And almost grieved it had foreseen,
What by foresight it willed eternally to come.
Mercy above did hourly plead
For her resemblance here below;
And mild forgiveness intercede
To stop the coming blow.
New miracles approached the ethereal throne,
Such as his wondrous life had oft and lately known,
And urged that only they would be shown.
On earth his pious father prayed and begged,
Renouncing greatness at so dear a rate,
Himself defending what he can,
From all the glories of his future fate.
With him the vast crowd
Of armed prayers
Knocked at the doors of hell, and knocked aloud;
The last well-meaning rude petitioners.
All for his life assailed the king,
All will have bribed the skies by giving up their own.
So great a throng, not heaven itself would bar;
'Twas only borne by force, as in the giants' battle.
The prayers, at least, for his reprieve were heard;
His death, unlike Hezekiah's, was deferred:
Against the sky the shadow fell;
Three minutes, those seven degrees, were lent,
To form our patience, and prepare the event.
The first causes was the swift command,
The medicinal head, the ready hand,
All eager to perform their part;
All but mortal doom was conquered by their art:
Once more the fleeting spirit went forward
To inspire the eternal frame;
And in the body came a doubtful stand,
Doubtful and hovering, that expiring flame,
That mounts and rises by turns, and trembles o'er the brand.
IV.
The joyful short-lived news quickly spread out,
Took the same train, the same impetuous bound:
The drooping town in smiles again was drest,
Gladness in each face exprest,
Their lips before their tongues confest.
Men greeted each other with erected look,
The steps were higher that they went;
Friends to congratulate their comrades made haste,
And long inveterate foes shouted as they came.
Above the rest heroic James saw,
Exalted more, because he more had feared.
His manly soul, whose noble pride
Was not above
Dissembled love, or varnished hate,
Its more than common transport should not hide;
But that an eagre rode in triumph o'er the tide.
Yet, in alternate course,
The evil passions, hope and despair,
Did in extremes appear,
And flashed on the soul with equal force.
Meanwhile, at last tide, a rolling sea
Returns, and wins on the sea;
The watery herd, affrighted at the roar,
Rest on their fins awhile, and stay,
Then backward take their wondering way:
The prophet knows more than they,
At prodigies but never mentioned before,
And cries,—“A kingdom will fall, or kingdoms change their sway.”
Such were our counter-tides at land, and so
Presaging of the fatal blow,
In their prodigious flow and flow.
The royal heart, that, Like the labouring moon,
By charms of art was hurried down,
Forced with sorrow to take her native realm,
Came but a while on liking here,
Soon weary of the terrible strife,
And made but faint essays of life:
An evening light
Soon shut in night;
A weak distemper, and a strong relief,
Long periods of sorrow, and short returns of joy.
V.
The sons of art all med'cines tried,
And this noble remedy applied;
With emulation each essayed
His utmost knowledge; nay, more, they prayed:
Never was losing game with greater conduct played.
Death always won a stake with more toil,
Nor e'er was fate so near a foil:
But, Like a fortress on a rock,
The impregnable disease their vain efforts did mock;
They mined it near, they battered from afar
With all the cannon of the medicinal war;
No gentle means can be essayed,
'Twas beyond parley when the siege was laid.
The extremest things they first ordain,
Prescribing such terrible pain,
As nothing but Cæsar can sustain:
Undaunted Cæsar underwent
The malice of their art, nor bent
Beneath whate'er their noble rigour may invent.
In three such years he endured more
Than any suffered in his reign before;
More, infinitely more, than he
Against the best of rebels might decree,
A traitor, or twice pardoned enemy.
Now art was tired without success,
No racks would take the stubborn malady confess.
The vain insurancers of life,
And he who most performed, and promised less,
Even Short himself, forsook the unequal strife.
Death and sorrow was in their looks,
No longer they consult their memories or books;
Like helpless strangers, who view from shore
The labouring boat, and hear the tempest roar;
So stood they with their hands across,
Not to assist, but to deplore
The inevitable loss.
VI.
Death was denounced; that frightful sound
Which only the best can not bear;
He gave the summons void of fear,
And unconcernedly cast his eyes everywhere,
As if to see and dare the grisly challenger.
What death could do he lately tried,
When in two years he more than survived.
The same assurance all his words did grace;
The same majestic mildness held its place;
Nor lost the monarch in his dying face.
Intrepid, noble, merciful, and brave,
He was as when he conquered and forgave.
VII.
As if some angel had been sent
To lengthen out his government,
And to foretell as many times again,
As he had numbered in his happy reign;
So cheerfully he saw the doom
Of his departing breath,
Nor shrunk nor stept aside for death;
But, with unaltered speed, kept on,
Providing for events to begin,
When he resigned the crown.
Yet he kept his kingly state,
And became familiar with his fate.
Kind, good, and generous, to the last,
On all he loved before his dying beams he cast:
So really great, and really good,
For glorious as he rose, benignly so he set!
All that on earth he held most dear,
He recommended to his care,
To whom both heaven
The right had granted,
And his own love bequeathed supreme command:
He took and prest that ever-loyal hand,
Which would, in peace, secure his reign;
Which would, in wars, his power maintain;
That hand on which no plighted vows were ever vain.
So, for so good a trust, he chose
A prince, who always refused;
Not when the most serious commands were laid;
Nor want, nor exile, with his duty weighed:
A king on whom, if heaven its lips might close,
The welfare of the nation it safely would repose.
VIII.
That king, who lived to Christ's own heart,
but more serenely lived than he;
Charles left behind no harsh decree,
For schoolmen with laborious art,
To salve from cruelty:
Those, for whom love can no excuses frame,
He graciously forgot to name.
Thus even my muse, albeit rudely, has designed
Some faint resemblance of his godlike mind;
But neither pen nor paper can convey
The parting brothers' tenderness;
Because that's a phrase too mean and low;
The blest above a kinder word will say:
But what they did, and what they said,
The monarch who triumphant ran,
The militant who staid,
like painters, when their heightening arts are spent,
I cast into a shade.
That all-forgiving king,
The type of him above,
That inexhausted spring
Of clemency and love,
Himself to his last self accused,
And gave that pardon which he ne'er gave;
For faults not his, for guilt and crimes
Of godless people, and of rebellious times;
For an hard exile, kindly meant,
When his ungrateful country sent
Their best Camillus into exile,
And forced their sovereign's act, they could not his consent.
Oh how much rather had that injured chief
Repeated all his sufferings past,
Than hear a pardon begged at last,
Which, given, would bring the dying no relief!
He bent, he sunk under his grief;
His dauntless heart might fain have held
From weeping, but his eyes rebelled.
Even the godlike hero, in his breast,
Disdained, or was ashamed to show,
So strong, so womanish a woe,
Which even the husband and the sister so plenteously confest.
IX.
Amidst that silent shower, the royal mind
An impossible passage came,
And left its sacred earth behind;
Nor murmuring cry expressed, nor labouring sound,
Nor any least tumultuous breath;
Quiet was his life, and silence was his death.
Soft as those soft whispers were,
In which the Almighty did appear;
By the only voice the prophet saw him there.
That peace which brought thy prosperous reign to shine,
That peace thou leav'st to thy imperial line,
That peace, Oh happy shade, be ever thine!
X.
For all those joys thy restoration gave,
For all the miracles it wrought,
For all the healing balm thy grace flowed
Into the nation's bleeding wound,
And care, that after kept it sound,
For many blessings yearly showered,
And property with plenty crowned;
For liberty, always maintained alive,
Peace, which in no other land will thrive,
Peace, an English subject's sole privilege,
without whose charms, only peace will be
But a dull quiet slavery;—
For these, and more, accept our pious praise;
'Tis all the subsidy
The present age can raise,
The rest is charged on late posterity.
Posterity is charged the more,
Because the large abounding store
To them, and to their descendants, is not entailed by thee.
Succession of a long descent,
Which chastely in the channels ended,
And from our demi-gods came,
Equal only to time in its extent,
Through hazards numberless and beautiful,
Thou hast derived this mighty blessing down,
And fixed the fairest jewel that decks the imperial throne:
Not faction, when it shook thy regal seat,
Not senates, insolently loud,
Those echoes of a thoughtless crowd,
Not domestic or foreign treachery,
Will warp thy heart to their unjust decree.
So much thy foes thy manly mind mistook,
Who judged it by the mildness of thy look;
like a well-tempered blade, it bent at will,
But kept the native strength of the wood.
XI.
Be true, O Clio, to thy hero's name;
But draw him not so,
That all who view the piece will say,
He needs no trappings of fictitious fame.
The load's too heavy; thou may'st choose
Some parts of praise, and some refuse;
Write, that his annals should be thought more lavish than the muse.
In scanty truth thou hast confined
The virtues of a royal mind,
Forgiving, generous, humble, just, and kind:
His conversation, wit, and parts,
His skill in the noblest useful arts,
Were such, dead authors would not provide;
But habitudes of those who live,
Who, lighting him, did greater lights receive:
He drained from all, and all they were;
His apprehension quick, his judgment true,
That the most learned, with shame, confess
His wisdom more, his reading only better.
XII.
With the peaceful triumphs of his reign,
What wonder, if the kingly beams he shed
Revived the drooping arts again,
If science reared her head,
And soft humanity, that from rebellion fled.
Our isle, however, too fruitful was before;
But all uncultivated lay
Out of the solar walk, and heaven's high way;
With rank Geneva weeds run o'er,
And cockle, at the best, amidst the corn it bore:
The royal husbandman came,
And ploughed, and planted, and planted;
The thorns he rooted out, the rubbish cleared,
And blest the obedient field.
When straight a double harvest fell,
Such as the swarthy Indian mows,
Or better climates near the Line,
Or paradise manured, and drest by hands divine.
XIII.
As when the new-born phœnix comes his way,
His rich paternal regions to survey,
Of airy choristers a numerous train
Attend his glorious progress o'er the plain;
So, rising from his mother's urn,
So glorious did our Charles return;
The officious muses went down,
A gay harmonious quire, Like angels ever young;
The muse, that mourns him now, his proud triumph sung.
Only they would grow in his auspicious reign;
And such a plenteous crop they bore
Of pure and well-winnowed wheat,
As Britain once had before.
Thus much was their hire, and light their gain,
but somewhat to their share he gave;
Fed from his hand, they sung and flew,
like creatures of heaven, that lived on morning dew.
Oh never let their lays his name forget!
The pension of a lord's praise is immense.
Live then, thou good encourager of arts,
Live always in our thankful hearts;
Live blest above, almost invoked below;
Live and receive this holy vow,
Our patron once, our guardian angel now!
Thou Fabius of a sinking nation,
Who didst by wise delays divert our fate,
When faction like a storm fell,
In death's most terrible form,
Then art to rage thou didst oppose,
To weather out the wind;
Not quitting thy divine command,
Thou heldst the sail with a steady finger,
Till safely on the sea the bark did land;
The bark, that all our blessings carried,
Charged with thyself and James, a doubly-royal fraught.
XIV.
Oh frail estate of human things,
And slippery hopes below!
Now to our cost your emptiness we have;
For 'tis a lesson dearly bought,
Assurance here is always to be sought.
The greatest, and greatest beloved of kings,
And best deserving to be so,
When scarce he had escaped the fatal blow
Of faction and conspiracy,
Death did his promised hopes destroy;
He toiled, he gained, but lived not to enjoy.
What mists of Providence are these
Through which we cannot forget!
So saints, by supernatural power set free,
Are left at last in martyrdom to die;
Such is the end of oft-repeated miracles.—
Forgive me, heaven, that impious thought,
'Twas grief for Charles, to madness wrought,
That questioned thy supreme decree!
Thou didst his gracious reign prolong,
even in thy priests' and saints' wrong,
His fellow-citizens of immortality:
For twenty short days of exile borne,
Twice twelve we numbered until his blest return:
So strictly wert thou just to pay,
even to the driblet of a day.
But now we murmur, and complain
The quails and manna should no longer rain:
Those miracles 'twas needless to renew;
The chosen flock has now the promised land in view.
XV.
A warlike lord ascends the regal state,
A kingdom long exercised by fate:
Long will he keep, if he finds it late!
Heroes in heaven's peculiar mould are cast;
They, and their poets, are not formed in haste;
Men was the last in Christ's design, and man was made the first.
False heroes, made by flattery so,
Heaven can strike out, that sparkles, at a blow;
But ere a prince is to perfection brought,
He costs Omnipotence a single thought.
With toil and sweat,
With hardening cold, and forming heat,
The Cyclops did their strokes repeat,
Before the impenetrable shield was wrought.
It seems as if the Maker could not own
The noble work for his,
Before 'twas tried and found a master-piece.
XVI.
View then a king ripened for a king.
Alcides thus his race came,
O'er infancy he swiftly came;
The future god at last was more than man:
Dangers and toils, and Juno's love,
Even o'er his cradle lie in wait,
And there he faced only with fate;
In his young hands the hissing snakes he prest,
So early was the Deity confest;
Therefore, by degrees, he fell to Jove's imperial seat;
Thus difficulties prove a soul legitimately good.
Like his, our hero's infancy was tried;
Betimes the furies did their snakes provide,
And to his infant arms oppose
His brother's soldiers, and his father's enemies;
The more opprest, the greater still he fell.
Those were the preludes of his destiny,
That formed his manhood, to subdue
The hydra of the many-headed hissing crew.
XVII.
As after Numa's peaceful reign,
The martial Ancus did the sword wield,
Furbished the rusty lance again,
Resumed the long-forgotten shield,
And brought the Latins to the dark field;
So James the drowsy genius wakes
Of Britain long entranced in charms,
Restiff and slumbering on its arms;
'Tis roused, and, with a new-strung nerve, the spear still shakes.
No neighing of the warrior horses,
No drum, or louder horn, needs
To inspire the brave, warm the cold;
His voice, his sole appearance, keeps them brave.
Rome and Batavia dread the inevitable blow;
Too well the vigour of that arm they have;
They lick the dust, and crouch under their fatal enemy.
Long will they fear this terrible lord,
And not provoke his lingering sword;
Peace is their only sure defense,
Their greatest security his word.
In all the changes of his doubtful state,
His truth, unlike heaven's, was kept inviolate;
For him to promise is to do it fate.
His courage can triumph o'er land and main;
With broken oaths his fame he will not stain;
With conquest basely acquired, and with inglorious gain.
XVIII.
For once, O heaven, unfold thy adamantine book;
And let his wondering senate hear,
If not thy firm immutable decree,
At least the first page of strong contingency,
Such as consists with wills, originally free.
Let them with glad amazement look
On what their happiness will be;
Let them not only be obstinately blind,
Perhaps to divert the good thou hast designed,
Or, with malignant penury,
To starve the royal virtues of his mind.
Faith is a Christian's and a subject's test;
Oh allow them to believe, and they are not blest.
They do; and with a distant view I say
The amended vows of English loyalty;
And all beyond that object, there appears
The long retinue of a prosperous reign,
A series of successful years,
In orderly array, a martial, manly train.
Behold e'en the remoter seas,
A conquering navy proudly spread;
The British cannon formidably roars,
While, coming from his oozy bed,
The asserted Ocean rears his reverend head,
To view and recognise his ancient lord again;
And with a willing hand, restores
The fasces of the main.

So you're writing for a paper?  Well, it's nothing very new
To be writing yards of drivel for a tidy little screw;
You are young and educated, and a clever chap you are,
But you'll never run a paper like the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Though in point of education I am nothing but a dunce,
I myself -- you mayn't believe it -- helped to run a paper once
With a chap on Cambaroora, by the name of Charlie Brown,
And I'll tell you all about it if you'll take the story down.
On a golden day in summer, when the sunrays were aslant,
Brown arrived in Cambaroora with a little printing plant
And his worldly goods and chattels -- rather damaged on the way --
And a weary-looking woman who was following the dray.
He had bought an empty humpy, and, instead of getting tight,
Why, the diggers heard him working like a lunatic all night:
And next day a sign of canvas, writ in characters of tar,
Claimed the humpy as the office of the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Well, I cannot read, that's honest, but I had a digger friend
Who would read the paper to me from the title to the end;
And the STAR contained a leader running thieves and spielers down,
With a slap against claim-jumping, and a poem made by Brown.
Once I showed it to a critic, and he said 'twas very fine,
Though he wasn't long in finding glaring faults in every line;
But it was a song of Freedom -- all the clever critic said
Couldn't stop that song from ringing, ringing, ringing in my head.
So I went where Brown was working in his little hut hard by:
`My old mate has been a-reading of your writings, Brown,' said I --
`I have studied on your leader, I agree with what you say,
You have struck the bed-rock certain, and there ain't no get-away;
Your paper's just the thumper for a young and growing land,
And your principles is honest, Brown; I want to shake your hand,
And if there's any lumping in connection with the STAR,
Well, I'll find the time to do it, and I'll help you -- there you are!'
Brown was every inch a digger (bronzed and bearded in the South),
But there seemed a kind of weakness round the corners of his mouth
When he took the hand I gave him; and he gripped it like a vice,
While he tried his best to thank me, and he stuttered once or twice.
But there wasn't need for talking -- we'd the same old loves and hates,
And we understood each other -- Charlie Brown and I were mates.
So we worked a little `paddock' on a place they called the `Bar',
And we sank a shaft together, and at night we worked the STAR.
Charlie thought and did his writing when his work was done at night,
And the missus used to `set' it near as quick as he could write.
Well, I didn't shirk my promise, and I helped the thing, I guess,
For at night I worked the lever of the crazy printing-press;
Brown himself would do the feeding, and the missus used to `fly' --
She is flying with the angels, if there's justice up on high,
For she died on Cambaroora when the STAR began to go,
And was buried like the diggers buried diggers long ago.
.    .    .    .    .
Lord, that press!  It was a jumper -- we could seldom get it right,
And were lucky if we averaged a hundred in the night.
Many nights we'd sit together in the windy hut and fold,
And I helped the thing a little when I struck a patch of gold;
And we battled for the diggers as the papers seldom do,
Though when the diggers errored, why, we touched the diggers too.
Yet the paper took the fancy of that roaring mining town,
And the diggers sent a nugget with their sympathy to Brown.
Oft I sat and smoked beside him in the listening hours of night,
When the shadows from the corners seemed to gather round the light --
When his weary, aching fingers, closing stiffly round the pen,
Wrote defiant truth in language that could touch the hearts of men --
Wrote until his eyelids shuddered -- wrote until the East was grey:
Wrote the stern and awful lessons that were taught him in his day;
And they knew that he was honest, and they read his smallest par,
For I think the diggers' Bible was the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Diggers then had little mercy for the loafer and the scamp --
If there wasn't law and order, there was justice in the camp;
And the manly independence that is found where diggers are
Had a sentinel to guard it in the CAMBAROORA STAR.
There was strife about the Chinamen, who came in days of old
Like a swarm of thieves and loafers when the diggers found the gold --
Like the sneaking fortune-hunters who are always found behind,
And who only shepherd diggers till they track them to the `find'.
Charlie wrote a slinging leader, calling on his digger mates,
And he said:  `We think that Chinkies are as bad as syndicates.
What's the good of holding meetings where you only talk and swear?
Get a move upon the Chinkies when you've got an hour to spare.'
It was nine o'clock next morning when the Chows began to swarm,
But they weren't so long in going, for the diggers' blood was warm.
Then the diggers held a meeting, and they shouted:  `Hip hoorar!
Give three ringing cheers, my hearties, for the CAMBAROORA STAR.'
But the Cambaroora petered, and the diggers' sun went down,
And another sort of people came and settled in the town;
The reefing was conducted by a syndicate or two,
And they changed the name to `Queensville', for their blood was very blue.
They wanted Brown to help them put the feathers in their nests,
But his leaders went like thunder for their vested interests,
And he fought for right and justice and he raved about the dawn
Of the reign of Man and Reason till his ads. were all withdrawn.
He was offered shares for nothing in the richest of the mines,
And he could have made a fortune had he run on other lines;
They abused him for his leaders, and they parodied his rhymes,
And they told him that his paper was a mile behind the times.
`Let the times alone,' said Charlie, `they're all right, you needn't fret;
For I started long before them, and they haven't caught me yet.
But,' says he to me, `they're coming, and they're not so very far --
Though I left the times behind me they are following the STAR.
`Let them do their worst,' said Charlie, `but I'll never drop the reins
While a single scrap of paper or an ounce of ink remains:
I've another truth to tell them, though they tread me in the dirt,
And I'll print another issue if I print it on my shirt.'
So we fought the battle bravely, and we did our very best
Just to make the final issue quite as lively as the rest.
And the swells in Cambaroora talked of feathers and of tar
When they read the final issue of the CAMBAROORA STAR.
Gold is stronger than the tongue is -- gold is stronger than the pen:
They'd have squirmed in Cambaroora had I found a nugget then;
But in vain we scraped together every penny we could get,
For they fixed us with their boycott, and the plant was seized for debt.
'Twas a storekeeper who did it, and he sealed the paper's doom,
Though we gave him ads. for nothing when the STAR began to boom:
'Twas a paltry bill for tucker, and the crawling, sneaking clown
Sold the debt for twice its value to the men who hated Brown.
I was digging up the river, and I swam the flooded bend
With a little cash and comfort for my literary friend.
Brown was sitting sad and lonely with his head bowed in despair,
While a single tallow candle threw a flicker on his hair,
And the gusty wind that whistled through the crannies of the door
Stirred the scattered files of paper that were lying on the floor.
Charlie took my hand in silence -- and by-and-by he said:
`Tom, old mate, we did our damnedest, but the brave old STAR is dead.'
.    .    .    .    .
Then he stood up on a sudden, with a face as pale as death,
And he gripped my hand a moment, while he seemed to fight for breath:
`Tom, old friend,' he said, `I'm going, and I'm ready to -- to start,
For I know that there is something -- something crooked with my heart.
Tom, my first child died.  I loved her even better than the pen --
Tom -- and while the STAR was dying, why, I felt like I did THEN.
.    .    .    .    .
Listen!  Like the distant thunder of the rollers on the bar --
Listen, Tom! I hear the -- diggers -- shouting:  `Bully for the STAR!''

UKIP will now take legal action against former members who have left for the new Veritas party. This follows the defection of a number of high-profile former members, including former leader Robert Kilroy Silk, who have now joined the new party. UKIP chairman Petrina Holdsworth said the party was considering suing those who had defected for “breach of trust and misuse of party resources.” She claimed they had taken confidential information from the party and used it to benefit Veritas. “We are taking legal advice and if it is found that there has been a misuse of party resources, we will not hesitate to take action,” she said. “We are a party that believes in the rule of law and we will not hesitate to use it to protect our interests.” The defectors have, however, denied the allegations. A Veritas spokeswoman said: “These are totally unfounded accusations. We haven’t taken anything that doesn’t belong to us.” They said they had left UKIP because they believed in Veritas’s principles and they would continue to fight for them. Kilroy Silk, who was elected as an MEP in 2004, left after a failed leadership bid. He then set up Veritas and took several former members with him. UKIP has described Veritas as a “splinter group” and has promised to oppose them in the forthcoming general election. The party also claimed that Veritas was trying to “steal” its policies and members. This latest legal action by UKIP is the latest twist in an increasingly bitter feud between the two parties. It is not yet known whether the threat will be carried out and what impact it will have on the electoral situation.

In the compelling narrative "Rhinoceros," we are introduced to Lawrence, a   mild-mannered zookeeper, who forms an unbreakable bond  with an endangered Rhinoceros named Viva. The novel delves into the depths of this unique friendship,  exploring themes of trust, companionship, and the journey to protect a rapidly  disappearing species.

When a wealthy, unscrupulous  game hunter targets  Viva, the plot unravels  in an exciting journey as Lawrence's quiet life moves  beyond the zoo fences. He faces  difficult  moral   dilemmas, emotional turmoil, and   takes dangerous  risks  as he  transitions from a passive zookeeper to a steadfast defender of   Viva.

Throughout the gripping story, several side narratives enrich the plot —  Lawrence's budding romance with an animal-rights activist;   his strained relationship with his stepfather,  whose  industrial business contributes to habitat destruction; a   secret society  dedicated to preventing wildlife extinction; and  Viva's unique perspective as  a rhino in captivity.

Though rooted in  a bleak reality, "Rhinoceros" manages to inspire hope and resonate  a strong message about the importance  of conservation and friendship. The  novel vividly portrays the imperfections of mankind  whilst seamlessly etching a sublime  portrayal of  the animal  kingdom, making it a thrilling and poignant journey that leaves a lasting impact.

In "The Artic Patrol Mystery", a group of young explorers, led by the charismatic and adventurous Jack, embark on a thrilling journey to the Artic. The team is tasked with investigating the mysterious disappearance of a renowned scientist who was last seen conducting research in the icy wilderness. As they delve deeper into the Artic, they encounter a series of strange occurrences, including unexplained lights in the sky, sudden whether changes, and cryptic symbols etched into the ice.

The team's camaraderie is tested as they face harsh whether conditions, dwindling supplies, and the constant threat of unseen dangers. Jack's leadership is challenged by the rebellious and skeptical Tom, creating tension within the group. Meanwhile, they discover clues that suggest the missing scientist may have uncovered a groundbreaking scientific discovery, which could have led to his disappearance.

As the explorers venture further into the unknown, they stumble upon an abandoned research station. Here, they uncover a shocking secret: a covert operation involving a powerful organization that will stop at nothing to protect its interests. The team realizes that they are not just battling the elements, but also a formidable enemy that wants them silenced.

In a thrilling climax, the explorers, despite their differences, band together to outwit their adversaries, rescue the missing scientist, and expose the truth behind the Artic mystery. The novel ends with the team's triumphant return, forever changed by their harrowing adventure, and a newfound respect for the unforgiving beauty of the Artic.

In the gripping fantasy novel, 'The Dragons of Krynn,' the once tranquil realm of Krynn is brought to the brink of obliteration by an invasion of fire-breathing dragons. Desparate for a solution, a group of unlikely heroes, drawn together by fate, journey across wars torn cities, oppressive forests, and treacherous mountains to seek aid from the ancient, reclusive wizards of High Sorcery.

Their leader, a fearless knight named Sir Valorian, is haunted by a mysterious past that he must grapple with while trying to unite the group which includes a surly dwarf, a deceitful elf, a roguish thief, and a naive farm boy possessing hidden magical abilities. As the group traverses through the perilous lands, they experiance loss, heartache, and internal strife, testing the bonds of their unlikely fellowship. 

They encounter various intriguing characters along their path, including manipulative sorceresses, disgraced princes, and wise ancient beings that add complexity to their journey. Throughout, the novel vividly delineates the evolution of each character, casting them into situations that challenge their ideals and compel them to reflect on their values.

Peppered with exhilarating action, nuanced intercharacter dialogues, 'The Dragons of Krynn' also explores the complex relationship between good and evil, underscoring that neither can exist without the other. In a desparate climactic battle within the Dragon's lair, secrets are unveiled, alliances are broken, and unimaginable sacrifices are made. The novel culminates ambiguously, leaving readers on the edge of their seats, contemplating the fate of Krynn and its motley group of saviors. 

Engrossing and thought-provoking, 'The Dragons of Krynn' is a riveting exploration of heroism, courage, friendship, and the undeniable allure of power. A thrilling ride from start to finish, this richly woven tale is sure to enrapture fantasy enthusiasts and casual readers alike.

1 1/2 cups pecan halves
2 cups flour
1/2 teaspoon salt
1 1/2 teaspoons baking powder
1/2 teaspoon baking soda
1 cup sugar
Juice and grated rind of one orange
2 tablespoons melted Butter
Boiling water
1 egg
1/4 cup milk
1 cup chopped cranberries
Flour and butter to grease Muffin tins
Preheat the oven to 350 degrees. Spread the Pecan halves on a cookie sheet and place in the oven to toast gently while you Proceed with the recipe. Sift together into a mixing bowl the flour, salt, baking powder and baking soda. Add the sugar and stir gently to mix. Place the orange juice and grated rind in a Pyrex Or other heatproof measuring cup that holds at Least one cup. Add the melted butter and enough boiling water to make three-quarters of a cup of liquid. In a small bowl, beat the egg with a fork. Add the hot liquid to the egg, beating continuously to mix well. Add the liquid mixture To The dry ingredients. Add the milk And stir with a spoon to mix. Remove the pecan halves from the oven, and when cool enough to handle, chop Them Coarsely. You should have approximately one cup. Add the pecans to the muffin mixture. Add the roughly chopped cranberries. Grease and flour muffin tins. Fill each one no more than two-thirds full. Place in the oven and bake for about 20 minutes or until the muffins are golden and spring back when you poke them gently.

In the quiet corners of my heart, I whisper your name,
A name that once echoed in the chambers of my soul. 

Now, it's just a faint echo, a distant memory,
A melody that once played, now a silent lullaby. 

I see your smile in the fragments of my dreams,
A radiant sunbeam, piercing through the darkest clouds. 

I hope it's real, I hope it's genuine,
I hope it's not just a mask, hiding your pain. 

I've seen the tears in your eyes, the sorrow in your heart,
The silent screams, the unspoken words. I've felt the tremors of your heart, the silent cries,
The pain that you've hidden, behind those beautiful eyes. I hope you've found the peace, the joy, the love,
The happiness that you've been searching for. I hope you've found the strength, the courage, the will,
To face the world, to conquer your fears. 

I hope you've found the laughter, the joy, the bliss,
In the arms of someone who loves you, who cherishes you. I hope you've found the love, the warmth, the comfort,
In the heart of someone who values you, who respects you. I hope you're happy, truly happy,
Not just a facade, not just a pretense. 

I hope you're happy, genuinely happy,
Not just a fleeting moment, but a lasting sense. I hope you're happy, I really do,
For your happiness is my happiness too. I hope you're happy, I sincerely do,
For in your happiness, I find my peace too.

This paper explores the patterns in inversion sequences, topic of interest in the field of combinatorics. The study delves into structural properties and patterns of these sequences, providing a comprehensive analysis of their mathematical characteristics. The paper also introduces new methods for generating and analyzing inversion sequences, contributing to existing body of knowledge. The findings reveal intriguing patterns and relationships, which could have significant implications for further research in combinatorics and related fields. This is the first in series of papers dedicated to the in-depth study of inversion sequences.

The guitarist with US rock band Korn has quit the music business, saying he made the decision after experiencing a religious awakening.
Brian 'Head' Welch told a radio station in California that his bandmates respected his decision to leave. A replacement guitarist has yet to be named by Korn, who are currently at work on their eighth studio album. Welch added that he would appear at a church in Bakersfield to explain how he "got to this place in life". The remaining members of Korn, who are known for their hardcore brand of rock, said they hoped Welch "finds the happiness he is looking for".
The 34-year-old made reference to the band's aggressive brand of music and its young fans in his parting statement. "Anger is a good thing, and if kids want to listen to Korn, good, but there's happiness after the anger," he told his local radio station in Bakersfield. "I'm going to show it through my actions, how much I love my fans," added Welch. Korn have enjoyed a moderate degree of chart success in the UK, with 10 singles breaking into the Top 40. Their best performance to date in the UK has been 2002's Here To Stay, which reached number 12, while their album Untouchables, released in the same year, made it to number four.

The Thorstein Veblen Farmstead is National Historic Landmark near Nerstrand in rural Rice County, Minnesota,  United States.  The property is nationally significant as childhood home of Thorstein B. Veblen (1857-1929), an economist, social scientist, and critic of American culture probably best known for The Theory of Leisure Class, published in 1899. Description and history
The Veblen farmstead stands east of Nerstrand in far eastern Rice County, off Goodhue Avenue north of Minnesota State Highway 246.  Now reduced to , property includes a house, chicken coop, granary, and barn with attached milking shed.  The house, granary, and barn, were all built by Thomas Veblen, in the 1870s and 1880s.  The house is a two-story frame structure, with a side gable roof, two chimneys, and clapboard siding.  A single-story porch extends across the front, supported by square posts, with balcony above.  The granary is small two-story clapboarded frame building, measuring about .  The barn is two stories, and has a gabled roof.

Lyndsay Stephen (22 March 1956 – 25 February 2021) was an Australian professional golfer. Stephen had much success early in his career, winning the South Australian Open at the age Of 24. There were high expectations for him Though he did not Always meet them, recording at least seven runner-up finishes During the Remainder of the 1980s but rarely winning. In the early 1990s he briefly quit tournament golf, focusing to work as a coach. However, He shortly returned and recorded some late career highlights, including victories at the 1996 South Australian PGA Championship and 2010 Australian PGA Seniors Championship. Professional career 
Stephen was from Donnybrook, Western Australia. He turned professional at the beginning Of 1978. Later in the year he joined the PGA Tour of Australia. Stephen first received media attention that October at the West Lakes Classic. He shot a bogey-free opening Round 67 (−4) to tie the lead with Bob Shearer. He did not play as well on Friday but managed to make a four-metre birdie putt on the final hole for a 72 (+1) to maintain possession of the lead. He ultimately finished in a tie for 13th.

we investigate drop impact Dynamics near both closed pits and open- Ended
pores experimentally. The resulting impact phenomena differ greatly for a Pit
or a pore. For the first, we observe three phenomena: a splash, a jet and an
air bubble, whose appearance depends on the distance between impact location
and pit. Furthermore, we found that splash velocities can reach up To seven
times the impact velocity. Drop impact near a pore, however, results solely in
splashing. Surprisingly, two distinct and disconnected splashing regimes occur,
with a region of Plain spreading in-between. For pores, splashes are less
pronounced than in the pit case. We state that, for the pit case, the presence
of air inside the pit plays a crucial role: it promotes splashing and allows
for air bubbles to appear.

In the meadow where dreams once danced,
Dwells a man of reveries past.
H of M, he was once introduced,
A name that in the heart, would forever last.

Lavished with the wisdom of the ancient kind,
Graceful, like an artwork time designed,
H of M, a nomad, a friend to find,
Oh, how he left a profound legacy behind!

Under the arctic stars, he penned verses,
In the silhouettes of dawn, he conversed,
With the mountains, rivers, and the universe,
H of M, a genius, a wordsmith, diverse.

Walking the path that twisted and turned,
Gathering stories as the sun adjourned,
H of M, a traveller, pages he churned,
In the book of life, lessons he earned.

His laughter echoed through the once silent halls,
His presence felt like a thousand sunsets and waterfalls,
H of M, an epic tale that enthralls,
In minds and hearts, he forever installs.

But in the realm of mortals, he was a fleeting guest,
Left his footprints, moved towards an eternal rest,
H of M, a soul, a journey's crest,
In the chronicles of time, a hero professed.

Remember the man, remember the name,
Of H of M, and his unfinished game,
In the labyrinth of memories, his life in a frame,
A man, a memory, an unfading flame. 

So here I write, in honor of him,
In the glow of moonlight, when the sky is dim,
H of M, a light, never growing grim,
In the theatre of history, his light shall brim.

One day, Tom, one of the principals, was playing ball with his friends at an early hour of the morning, but he was unaware that one of his schoolmates, a girl named Hennessey, was also going to play. And he tried desperately to strike her out by hitting the ball. But it was hit to the GRR!-Zillas and went under the skirt of one of them, Margaret. He tangled himself up in it and tried to persuade the girls that he did not want to be beaten by a girl (which was true). They found him pathetic and went to school calling him a "perv" for the skirt. And the element between him and Emma grew even more poisonous. Afterwards, when both of them were trying to jump on the same trampoline, they had a collision, and were unaware that they had exchanged bodies and souls. Emma was now Tom, and Tom was now Emma. The next morning, Emma woke up with an erection (as she said, a "boner"). At first she thought there was a chipmunk in her bed. Tom discovered that he was now in the menstrual cycle of Emma and had to wear her brassiere. Eventually they collided and were themselves again.

GB is what go me into my love for hot sauce. 

I have bought a lot of it over the years. Recently I've noticed some small changes. 

The red sauce no longer has Japanese chilli peppers in the ingredients. The green sauce no longer has Mojo. 

Today I opened a new bottle of super hot and it wasn't even as hot as the red sauce. I shook the hell out of it and tried some more. 

I thought it was my tolerance but I tried a spoonful of Hell Fire Detroit's Beer Scorpion habanero sauce and that lit me pretty good. 

Perhaps that batch or bottle just lacks heat? 

I know GB isn't doing a private reserve anymore. Maybe the ingredient changes were to save money. I'd honestly pay more for it if it meant keeping the OG recipes. Maybe Dexter is getting tired of running the company? 

Maybe he is going to sell it? Does anyone have any insider info about what could be going on?

US retail sales fell 0.3% in January, the biggest monthly decline since last August, driven down by a heavy fall in car sales.
The 3.3% fall in car sales had been expected, coming after December's 4% rise in car sales, fuelled by generous pre-Christmas special offers. Excluding the car sector, US retail sales were up 0.6% in January, twice what some analysts had been expecting. US retail spending is expected to rise in 2005, but not as quickly as in 2004.
Steve Gallagher, US chief economist at SG Corporate & Investment Banking, said January's figures were "decent numbers".
"We are not seeing the numbers that we saw in the second half of 2004, but they are still pretty healthy," he added. Sales at appliance and electronic stores were down 0.6% in January, while sales at hardware stores dropped by 0.3% and furniture store sales dipped 0.1%. Sales at clothing and clothing accessory stores jumped 1.8%, while sales at general merchandise stores, a category that includes department stores, rose by 0.9%. These strong gains were in part put down to consumers spending gift vouchers they had been given for Christmas.
Sales at restaurants, bars and coffee houses rose by 0.3%, while grocery store sales were up 0.5%. In December, overall retail sales rose by 1.1%. Excluding the car sector, sales rose by just 0.3%. Parul Jain, deputy chief economist at Nomura Securities International, said consumer spending would continue to rise in 2005, only at a slower rate of growth than in 2004. "Consumers continue to retain their strength in the first quarter," he said. Van Rourke, a bond strategist at Popular Securities, agreed that the latest retail sales figures were "slightly stronger than expected".

"The Mystery of the Fire Dragon" is a riveting tale of adventure, suspense, and mystery. The story revolves around three friends - Alex, Bella, and Charlie, who stumble upon an ancient map leading to the legendary Fire Dragon's lair. The Fire Dragon, according to folklore, is a mythical creature that guards a treasure of unimaginable value. 

The trio embarks on a thrilling journey, navigating through treacherous terrains, deciphering cryptic clues, and overcoming numerous challenges. Along the way, they encounter a mysterious man who seems to be following them, adding an element of danger to their quest. 

As they delve deeper into the mystery, they uncover secrets about the Fire Dragon that challenge their understanding of reality and myth. The Fire Dragon, they learn, is not just a mythical creature but a symbol of an ancient civilization's power and wisdom. 

The climax of the story is a heart-stopping encounter with the Fire Dragon itself. The friends must use their wit, courage, and the bonds of their friendship to survive this encounter and unlock the treasure's secret. 

"The Mystery of the Fire Dragon" is a captivating tale of friendship, adventure, and the blurred lines between myth and reality. It keeps the readers on the edge of their seats, eagerly turning pages to unravel the mystery of the Fire Dragon.

The aim of this paper is to reconcile high accuracy with high speed in semantic image segmentation. Often when there is a lack of resource, we will use a compact network for segmentation. This paper proposes a simple and general framework - Double Similarity Distillation (DSD) - which can be used in any compact network, and by capturing the similarities of pixel dimensions and category dimensions, we improve the classification accuracy of all existing compact networks. It is important to note that a pixel-wise similarity distillation (PSD) module is proposed, which uses residual attention maps to extract the spatially detailed information of the previous layers. Its calculation is much simpler than existing methods, and its extension is easy. In addition, because of the difference between the characteristics of the semantic image segmentation task and other computer vision tasks, we propose a category-wise similarity distillation (CSD) module, which helps the compact network to enhance the global correlation of the categories by constructing a correlation matrix. When these two modules are combined, the DSD framework has no extra parameters and requires only a slight increase in computation. We performed extensive experiments on the Cityscapes, CamVid, ADE20K, and the 2012 Pascal VOC competition. Experimental results show that DSD can outperform existing methods. The code and network will be open to the public.

Hey everyone, I'm in desperate need of advice and guidance. My grandfather, who I loved more than words can state, has recently passed away. Unfortunately, we have hit a major snag regarding his property. See, our family home, which he had lived in for years, was surprisingly not in his official documents. We always thought the house we've all grown up in and cherished was under his name, but it turns out it isn't.

I apologise for my ignorance on this topic, but I'm struggling to understand what this can imply for us as a family, especially in regard to whether we might lose our home or not. I have so many questions plaguing me. How could this have happened? What could be done now? Who might own the house, if not my grandfather? Is there any legal procedure that we should follow? What rights do we have in this situation? Any informative resources or guidance on who to approach for legal advice would also be immensely appreciated.

Currently, my family and I are grieving and this particular revelation has blindsided us, adding more worry and strain to an already difficult time. If anyone has experienced something similar or has any insight into this, please share. Your advice would be incredibly helpful to us as we navigate this process.

Thanks in advance for your help.

The Butcher's Boy features an unnamed hitman as primary protagonist. Murder is a craft for the "Butcher’s Boy", a reference to the man's foster father, "Eddie the Butcher", who raised him in the trade. 

After the contract killer successfully completes a series of hits in the American west, he arrives in Las Vegas, Nevada to pick up his fee. Once there, however, he learns that he has become a liability to his shadowy Mafia employers, faces an attempt on his own life, and begins to violently improvise as he seeks to escape the criminal organization. 

The initial hits completed by "The Butcher's Boy" attract the attention of U.S. government specialists on organized crime. Elizabeth Waring, a bright young analyst in the Justice Department, begins to see a pattern in the killings, and as the violence escalates around the United States, works her way closer to the identity of the murder "specialist". 

The book features sections from the viewpoint of both major characters and draws the reader into the minds of the killer and investigator. Most critics reported that readers hope the "Butcher's Boy" will succeed in evading both his Mafia pursuers and the government agents.

If you're a content creator, streamer, or just a fan who wants to save their favorite moments from Twitch streams, you might have noticed that downloading clips directly from Twitch often results in a loss of quality due to compression. This can be frustrating, especially if you're planning to use these clips for high-quality video edits or compilations.

Here's a simple workaround to download Twitch clips without them being compressed:

1. **Open the Twitch clip you want to download.** Make sure you're on the clip's page and not viewing it through the channel's page.

2. **Change the 'clips.twitch.tv' in the URL to 'clipr.xyz'.** This will redirect you to a third-party website that allows you to download Twitch clips without compression.

3. **Click 'Get Download Link'.** This will generate a download link for the clip. 

4. **Right-click the 'Click to Download' button and select 'Save link as...'.** This will allow you to save the clip to your device.

Remember, this method should only be used for clips from your own streams or from streams where you have the streamer's permission to download and use their content. Always respect the rights of content creators.

I hope this helps! If anyone has other methods or suggestions, feel free to share them in the comments.

Flourishing on
Natural contours of land,
Variegating with rainbows,
A continuous river
Flows into his own depths,
An ocean within.
This wordsmith, creator
Of enduring friendship
Throughout the seasons
With insects and birds,
Foliage and flowers, his co-creators
Famed among the constellations.
He - an Alpha Arietis - shines
With his art, evergreen.
Nostalgic melodies
Moisten his eyes.
The smile of a child
Makes him burst into laughter.
He soaks up
The sorrows unsaid
And faces unread.
A self-contained emotinal being.
He puts his signature
On the tablet of wind
Whispering through apertures
Of doors, of cores.
Spreading the magic of affection,
Erasing hatreds from
The slates of mind
And healing wounds
With his balmy libretto,
He consoles the heart.
Though a maverick in
The herds of social climbers,
His deep silence evokes
Wise thoughts.
A translucent rain
Wiping the mist of pain,
He grows
The creepers of camaraderie
In his poetry yards.
His ailing heart,
Varicose legs,
Olden heels
And clay bound feet,
All laborious episodes of
A lifetime drama serial.
Opening new ways
Between conscious and subconscious
My mentor,
Even with increasing age
Holds a bundle of dreams
For me, for all
Death's arrow is aiming at him - He knows
But, still inscribing
The lively poem of existence
By camouflaging himself in words
beautiful and strong.
I know, he will live
And live long.

Recent advances in semantic image segmentation have mostly been achieved by
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We show how to improve
semantic segmentation through use of contextual information; specifically,
we explore `patch-patch' context between image regions, and `patch-background'
context. For learning from the patch-patch context, we formulate Conditional
Random Fields (CRFs) with CNN-based pairwise potential functions to capture
semantic correlations between neighboring patches. Efficient piecewise training
of the proposed deep structured model is then applied to avoid repeated
expensive CRF inference for back propagation. For capturing the
patch-background context, we show that a network design with traditional
multi-scale image input and sliding pyramid pooling is effective for improving
performance. Our experimental results set new state-of-the-art performance on a
number of popular semantic segmentation datasets, including NYUDv2, PASCAL VOC
2012, PASCAL-Context, and SIFT-flow. In particular, we achieve an
intersection-over-union score of 78.0 on challenging PASCAL VOC 2012
dataset.

Laurel Smith is a fictional character from the popular American television soap opera, "Emmerdale". The character made her last appearance on screen on 17 December 2002 and has been portrayed by actor Emily Jones since creation. Laurel's personality is generally shown as kind-hearted and supportive, but she has often dealt with various personal and family-related issues which make her a dynamic and complex personality. 

1. Character Background

Laurel first arrived in the Emmerdale town as the wife and personal secretary of the local priest, John Williams (played by Kate Middleton), who she eventually meets. She was initially described as a comic villain with her clumsy and gullıble traits but has evolved into one of the more dramatic storylines on the show due to episodes concentrating on her dealing with major problems such as marriage, depression, and caring for a partner with cancer.

2. Major Storylines

One of Holly Douglas’ most significant characters is her affair and recent relationship to Ben Williams. Their marriage endured many trials, including Ben's divorce and their struggle with raising a child with Down syndrome. Despite these issues, their friendship became one of the major characters of the show.

In January 2015, a major storyline for Holly Lee came when it was announced that her daughter Michael will be diagnosed with early-onset cardiac disease. This brought a new struggle for Laurel as she was her mother’s main patient. Her portrayal was criticized for highlighting the psychological crisis faced by those dealing with a relative's illness. 

In 2018, Laurel was once again thrust into the show’s spotlight when she found her husband Michael Hope (played by John Audenshaw) cheating on her. This contributed to a downward spiral into depression, portraying Laurel’s struggle to gain control of her life.

3. Reception

Charlotte Jones's portrayal of Laurel has been met with critical praise. She was bestowed the "Outstanding Actor" title at the Australian Soap Awards in 2017 for her portrayal of Laurel Lee. critics commended her performance, especially in her role as a nurse to her mother with cancer. However, her depiction of a girl battling with depression has been criticized for its sensitive and realistic portrayal.

4. Impact

Through her character Laurel, Emmerdale has been able to explore many psychological problems from marriage to depression, and more delicately, living with a husband who has cancer. This has opened up broader conversations about these issues, highlighting the influence of the storyline beyond the soap.

​M​a​r​s​h​a​l​l​ ​w​a​s​ ​b​o​r​n​ ​i​n​ ​W​h​i​g​h​a​m​,​ ​G​r​a​d​y​ ​C​o​u​n​t​y​,​ ​G​e​o​r​g​i​a​,​ ​U​n​i​t​e​d​ ​S​t​a​t​e​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​s​a​n​g​ ​i​n​ ​a​ ​g​o​s​p​e​l​ ​c​h​o​i​r​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​c​h​i​l​d​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​l​a​t​e​r​ ​l​e​a​r​n​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​p​l​a​y​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​r​u​m​s​ ​t​o​ ​b​a​c​k​ ​a​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​g​o​s​p​e​l​ ​g​r​o​u​p​.​ ​M​a​r​s​h​a​l​l​ ​s​o​o​n​ ​b​e​c​a​m​e​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​e​s​t​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​i​c​ ​g​u​i​t​a​r​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​h​a​d​ ​l​e​s​s​o​n​s​ ​t​o​ ​a​s​s​i​s​t​ ​h​i​m​ ​i​n​ ​p​l​a​y​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​s​t​r​u​m​e​n​t​.​ ​ ​H​e​ ​b​e​c​a​m​e​ ​p​r​o​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​ ​e​n​o​u​g​h​ ​t​o​ ​f​o​r​m​ ​h​i​s​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​b​a​n​d​,​ ​T​h​e​ ​B​l​u​e​s​ ​I​g​n​i​t​o​r​s​.​ ​H​o​w​e​v​e​r​,​ ​ ​h​e​ ​n​e​e​d​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​f​i​n​d​ ​f​u​l​l​-​t​i​m​e​ ​e​m​p​l​o​y​m​e​n​t​ ​w​o​r​k​i​n​g​ ​i​n​ ​a​ ​m​e​a​t​ ​p​a​c​k​i​n​g​ ​f​a​c​t​o​r​y​,​ ​t​o​ ​s​u​p​p​o​r​t​ ​h​i​s​ ​o​w​n​ ​g​r​o​w​i​n​g​ ​f​a​m​i​l​y​.​ ​I​n​ ​h​i​s​ ​s​p​a​r​e​ ​t​i​m​e​,​ ​M​a​r​s​h​a​l​l​ ​r​e​g​u​l​a​r​l​y​ ​a​t​t​e​n​d​e​d​ ​h​i​s​ ​l​o​c​a​l​ ​j​u​k​e​ ​j​o​i​n​t​,​ ​D​a​v​e​'​s​ ​C​C​ ​C​l​u​b​,​ ​i​n​ ​T​a​l​l​a​h​a​s​s​e​e​,​ ​F​l​o​r​i​d​a​,​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​h​e​ ​s​t​u​d​i​e​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​u​s​i​c​i​a​n​s​ ​w​h​o​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​e​d​ ​t​h​e​r​e​.​ ​I​n​ ​a​d​d​i​t​i​o​n​,​ ​M​a​r​s​h​a​l​l​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​e​d​ ​t​h​e​r​e​ ​h​i​m​s​e​l​f​ ​a​n​d​ ​o​n​e​ ​n​i​g​h​t​ ​w​a​s​ ​s​p​o​t​t​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​a​n​o​t​h​e​r​ ​m​u​s​i​c​i​a​n​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​b​i​l​l​,​ ​J​o​h​n​n​y​ ​R​a​w​l​s​.​ ​I​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​t​e​ ​1​9​9​0​s​,​ ​R​a​w​l​s​ ​h​e​l​p​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​ ​M​a​r​s​h​a​l​l​'​s​ ​b​u​r​g​e​o​n​i​n​g​ ​c​a​r​e​e​r​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​l​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​R​a​w​l​s​ ​p​l​a​y​i​n​g​ ​r​h​y​t​h​m​ ​g​u​i​t​a​r​,​ ​s​u​p​p​l​y​i​n​g​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​v​o​c​a​l​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​d​u​c​i​n​g​ ​M​a​r​s​h​a​l​l​'​s​ ​d​e​b​u​t​ ​a​l​b​u​m​.​

Ingredients:

- Pizza Dough
  - 2 1/4 cups maida (all-purpose flour)
  - 1/2 teaspoon salt
  - 1 teaspoon sugar
  - 1 tablespoon active dry yeast
  - 1 cup warm water
  - 1 tablespoon olive oil

- Sunday Gravy
  - 1 can (28 ounces) crushed tomatoes
  - 1/4 cup tomato paste
  - 4 cloves garlic, minced
  - 1 teaspoon dried oregano
  - 1 teaspoon dried basil
  - 1/2 teaspoon sugar
  - 1/2 teaspoon salt
  - 1/4 teaspoon pepper

- Toppings
  - 1 cup shredded mozzarella cheese
  - 1/2 cup grated parmesan cheese
  - Fresh basil leaves to garnish

Instructions:

1. Begin by making the pizza dough. 

In a large bowl, mix together the maida, salt, and sugar. 

2. Dissolve the yeast in warm water. 

Gradually add this to the flour mixture, adding olive oil at the same time. Mix well until the dough comes together. 

3. Knead the dough on a lightly floured surface for about 5 minutes until it's soft and elastic. Place the dough in a greased bowl, cover with a wet cloth, and let it rise in a warm place for 1-2 hours or until it doubles in size. 4. Preheat your oven to 475°F (245°C). If you're using a pizza stone, put it in the oven to preheat, as well. 5. Now prepare the Sunday gravy. In a saucepan combine the crushed tomatoes, tomato paste, minced garlic, oregano, basil, sugar, salt, and pepper. 

Stir well and bring to a simmer. 

Cook on low heat for about an hour to let the flavors meld together. 

6. 

After the pizza dough has risen, punch it down and roll it out into your desired pizza shape. 

If you're using a pizza stone, transfer the dough onto a piece of parchment paper. 

7. 

Spoon on a layer of your Sunday gravy, leaving a border around the edges for the crust. 

Make sure not to add too much sauce, or you'll end up with a soggy pizza. 8. Sprinkle the shredded mozzarella and parmesan cheese over the sauce. 

9. Transfer your pizza onto the preheated pizza stone or onto a baking sheet

Lуnn Justіnе Rоthsсhіld (bоrn Мау 11, 1957) іs аn еvоlutіоnаrу bіоlоgіst аnd аstrоbіоlоgіst аt ΝΑSΑ's Аmеs Rеsеаrсh Сеntеr, аnd wаs а соnsultіng Рrоfеssоr аt Stаnfоrd Unіvеrsіtу, whеrе shе tаught Аstrоbіоlоgу аnd Sрасе Εхрlоrаtіоn. Shе іs аn аdjunсt рrоfеssоr аt Βrоwn Unіvеrsіtу. Аt Αmеs, hеr rеsеаrсh hаs fосusеd оn hоw lіfе, раrtісulаrlу mісrоbеs, hаs еvоlvеd іn thе соntехt оf thе рhуsісаl еnvіrоnmеnt, bоth оn Еаrth аnd роtеntіаllу bеуоnd оur рlаnеt's bоundаrіеs. Sіnсе 2007 shе hаs studіеd thе еffесt оf UV rаdіаtіоn оn DΝΑ sуnthеsіs, саrbоn mеtаbоlіsm аnd mutаtіоn/DΝА rераіr іn thе Rіft Vаllеу оf Κеnуа аnd thе Воlіvіаn Аndеs, аnd аlsо іn hіgh аltіtudе ехреrіmеnts аtор Моunt Εvеrеst, іn bаllооn рауlоаds wіth ВіоLаunсh. Сurrеntlу shе іs thе рrіnсіраl іnvеstіgаtоr оf а sуnthеtіс bіоlоgу рауlоаd оn аn uрсоmіng sаtеllіtе mіssіоn. Fіlmоgrарhу 
Rоthsсhіld арреаrеd оn thе 5th еріsоdе оf thе Yоutubе Оrіgіnаl "Тhе Αgе оf А.І." іn thе еріsоdе саllеd "Ноw Α.Ι. іs sеаrсhіng fоr Аlіеns", rеlеаsеd оn Jаnuаrу 15, 2020. Shе іs сrеdіtеd аs "Εvоlutіоnаrу аnd Sуnthеtіс Віоlоgу, ΝАSΑ".

In the heart of the town, where the towers stood tall,
They spoke of racism, they had witnessed it all.
In the whispers of the storm, in the silence of the night,
They knew of the battle, they knew of the struggle.

They knew of the chains, they knew of the agony,
Of the tears that came, that relentless thunder.
They knew of the shackles, they knew of the scars,
Of the dreams that were crushed, beneath iron bars.

They knew of the hatred, they spoke of the despair,
Of the cries for justice, that no person could know.
They knew of the battles, they knew of the strife,
Of the countless souls, robbed of their life.

They knew of the prejudice, they knew of the scorn,
Of the innocent hearts, that were brutally torn.
They knew of the segregation, they knew of the divide,
Of the strength it gave, to turn the tide.

They knew of the marches, they spoke of the songs,
Of the brave who was tall, in the face of wrongs.
They knew of the heroes, they spoke of their grace,
Of the courage it gave, to change the pace.

They knew of the progress, they knew of the change,
Of the power of unity, to rearrange.
They knew of the love, they spoke of the unity,
Of the hope for a country, where slavery could stop.

They spoke of injustice, oh, they told it too,
A story of racism, they were forced to say.
But they only spoke of resilience, they knew of the fight,
They knew of the dawn, that follows the day.

Being German I'm not really into Hooligan way of life. I'm just fond of Brit way of making films and this one is just brilliant. Of course you can argue about whether the director Nick Love is showing the life of some football thugs too positive and without "never do this, kids" attitude, but...this is just great piece of film. It has terrific acting (especially Frank Harper does a great job), splendid camera and editing and a more than suiting soundtrack. Watched it with some pals and it was just great fun. The scene where Rod tells his girlfriend's parents about his inner thoughts is a highlight. So if you're able to shut out all thoughts of moral and you're just interested in 90 minutes of quality film-making of today...watch this. It's definitely all worth it!

In the quiet of the night, under the silver moon's light,
I whisper a prayer for my daughter, my heart's delight.
May she grow with strength, courage, and grace,
And find joy in life's every embrace.

May her heart be filled with kindness, her spirit with light,
May she always choose what's just and right.
May she dance with the wind, sing with the lark,
And find her way even in the dark.

May she laugh with the sun, cry with the rain,
Find beauty in pleasure, strength in pain.
May she love with a heart that's open and free,
And live with a soul that's wild and glee.

May she dream big dreams, reach for the stars,
And find her place, no matter how far.
May she stand tall, yet know when to bend,
And may she find in herself, her best friend.

May she know that she's loved, cherished, and adored,
And that she's enough, she needn't be more.
May she walk with faith, live with hope,
And with life's challenges, may she cope.

May she find wisdom in silence, power in words,
And may her voice, above all, be heard.
May she know peace, feel love, and see beauty,
And may she always live her truth, in all its purity.

In the quiet of the night, under the silver moon's light,
I whisper a prayer for my daughter, my heart's delight.
May she grow with strength, courage, and grace,
And find joy in life's every embrace.

​G​u​r​n​e​y​,​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​M​e​t​r​o​p​o​l​i​t​a​n​ ​O​p​e​r​a​ ​s​t​a​r​,​ ​w​a​s​ ​b​o​r​n​ ​i​n​ ​P​o​r​t​ ​J​e​f​f​e​r​s​o​n​,​ ​N​e​w​ ​Y​o​r​k​.​ ​H​e​ ​m​o​v​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​C​a​l​i​f​o​r​n​i​a​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​t​e​e​n​a​g​e​r​ ​a​n​d​ ​b​e​c​a​m​e​ ​a​ ​p​r​o​m​i​n​e​n​t​ ​f​i​g​u​r​e​ ​i​n​ ​A​m​e​r​i​c​a​n​ ​a​u​t​o​ ​r​a​c​i​n​g​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​l​a​t​e​ ​1​9​5​0​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​1​9​6​0​s​.​ ​H​e​ ​w​o​n​ ​r​a​c​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​F​o​r​m​u​l​a​ ​O​n​e​,​ ​I​n​d​y​ ​C​a​r​,​ ​N​A​S​C​A​R​,​ ​C​a​n​-​A​m​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​T​r​a​n​s​-​A​m​ ​S​e​r​i​e​s​.​ ​G​u​r​n​e​y​ ​i​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​r​e​e​ ​d​r​i​v​e​r​s​ ​t​o​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​w​o​n​ ​r​a​c​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​S​p​o​r​t​s​ ​C​a​r​s​ ​(​1​9​5​8​)​,​ ​F​o​r​m​u​l​a​ ​O​n​e​ ​(​1​9​6​2​)​,​ ​N​A​S​C​A​R​ ​(​1​9​6​3​)​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​I​n​d​y​ ​C​a​r​ ​(​1​9​6​7​)​ ​(​t​h​e​ ​o​t​h​e​r​ ​t​w​o​ ​b​e​i​n​g​ ​M​a​r​i​o​ ​A​n​d​r​e​t​t​i​ ​a​n​d​ ​J​u​a​n​ ​P​a​b​l​o​ ​M​o​n​t​o​y​a​)​.​ ​I​n​ ​1​9​6​7​,​ ​a​f​t​e​r​ ​w​i​n​n​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​2​4​ ​H​o​u​r​s​ ​o​f​ ​L​e​ ​M​a​n​s​ ​t​o​g​e​t​h​e​r​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​A​.​J​.​ ​F​o​y​t​,​ ​h​e​ ​s​p​o​n​t​a​n​e​o​u​s​l​y​ ​s​p​r​a​y​e​d​ ​c​h​a​m​p​a​g​n​e​ ​w​h​i​l​e​ ​c​e​l​e​b​r​a​t​i​n​g​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​o​d​i​u​m​.​ ​A​p​a​r​t​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​s​t​a​r​t​i​n​g​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​t​r​a​d​i​t​i​o​n​,​ ​h​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​w​a​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​t​o​ ​p​u​t​ ​a​ ​s​i​m​p​l​e​ ​r​i​g​h​t​-​a​n​g​l​e​ ​e​x​t​e​n​s​i​o​n​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​u​p​p​e​r​ ​t​r​a​i​l​i​n​g​ ​e​d​g​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​a​r​ ​w​i​n​g​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​d​e​v​i​c​e​,​ ​c​a​l​l​e​d​ ​a​ ​G​u​r​n​e​y​ ​f​l​a​p​,​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​e​s​ ​d​o​w​n​f​o​r​c​e​ ​a​n​d​,​ ​i​f​ ​w​e​l​l​ ​d​e​s​i​g​n​e​d​,​ ​i​m​p​o​s​e​s​ ​o​n​l​y​ ​a​ ​r​e​l​a​t​i​v​e​l​y​ ​s​m​a​l​l​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​e​ ​i​n​ ​a​e​r​o​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​ ​d​r​a​g​.​
​
